Title: The plastid cysteine synthase complex regulates ABA biosynthesis and stomatal closure in Arabidopsis

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for stomatal response to exogenous/xylem-delivered ABA, but not for sulfate-, CLE25-, OPDA-, or short-term high-light-induced closure, indicating guard-cell autonomous ABA biosynthesis is sufficient for early responses
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. abcg40 mutants are insensitive to applied ABA in epidermal peels and petiole-feeding assays. 2. The same mutants still close stomata in response to sulfate, CLE25, OPDA, and short-term high light. 3. These results support that early stress-triggered ABA is produced inside guard cells rather than requiring import.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; epidermal peel and petiole-feeding stomatal assays; pRAB18::GFP reporter analysis
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA treatment; sulfate; CLE25; OPDA; high light stress
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Highlights that engineering local ABA biosynthesis may bypass dependence on ABA import for rapid stomatal closure
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used to distinguish guard-cell ABA import from guard-cell ABA biosynthesis in the stress response.

Common_Genes.Source_Species_Variety: Ws for opr3-1; Col-related allele for opr3-3 not specified
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of OPR3 causes constitutively closed stomata through endogenous OPDA accumulation that activates the CYP20-3/pCSC/ABA pathway
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. opr3 mutants display constitutively closed stomata, elevated guard-cell ABA reporter signal, and increased cysteine. 2. This phenotype is independent of JA-Ile sensing but is suppressed by aba3, pCSC mutations, or cyp20-3. 3. OPR3 therefore acts as a negative modulator of the OPDA-pCSC-ABA axis by consuming OPDA.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; stomatal aperture assays; pRAB18::GFP ABA reporter; genetic epistasis with cyp20-3, serat2;1, oastlb, and aba3
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Endogenous oxylipin status; high light-related OPDA biology
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Infertility of JA-Ile-deficient mutants noted for seed production
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Demonstrates that manipulating OPDA turnover can alter ABA-mediated stomatal behavior, though pleiotropic oxylipin effects and fertility issues are cautions
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Total leaf OPDA/ABA steady-state changes were not always significant, indicating local guard-cell signaling is important.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of peroxisomal OPDA import causes constitutively closed stomata, consistent with OPDA accumulation activating the OPDA-pCSC-ABA axis
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pxa1-1 mutants, which accumulate OPDA under non-stressed conditions, show constitutively closed stomata. 2. This supports the model that endogenous OPDA accumulation can drive ABA-mediated stomatal closure. 3. PXA1 thereby modulates the availability of OPDA for the pCSC signaling axis.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping of stomatal aperture; pathway inference from known OPDA transport role
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Contrasted with jassy, which did not show stomatal phenotype, suggesting backup plastid export exists.

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Cysteine biosynthesis linked to ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Rate-limiting upstream step for cysteine supply
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes formation of O-acetylserine from serine and acetyl-CoA; its activity is stimulated by CSC formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies cysteine precursor pool required for ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for sulfate-, CLE25-, OPDA-, and high light-induced ABA accumulation and stomatal closure; loss increases soil-drying sensitivity
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SERAT2;1 is an essential plastid pCSC subunit required for sulfate- and CLE25-induced ABA reporter activation and stomatal closure. 2. SERAT2;1 is also required for OPDA/high light-triggered ABA accumulation and stomatal closure. 3. Constitutive pCSC activation depends on SERAT2;1 to elevate OAS/cysteine and drive drought-tolerant partial stomatal closure.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; stomatal aperture assays; pRAB18::GFP ABA reporter; double-mutant genetic tests; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control and rate-limiting step for cysteine supply
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Soil drying; sulfate feeding; CLE25 treatment; OPDA treatment; high light stress
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Activation of the SERAT2;1-containing pCSC is a promising engineering target to enhance drought resilience by boosting guard-cell ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: SERAT2;1 is the limiting SERAT isoform for pCSC formation despite low contribution to total leaf SERAT activity.

Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Present in plastids/pCSC-relevant tissues including guard cells
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Cysteine biosynthesis linked to ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: In free form catalyzes incorporation of sulfide into O-acetylserine to form cysteine; in the CSC acts as a regulatory subunit and is inactivated while stimulating SERAT activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies cysteine directly required as sulfur donor for ABA biosynthesis via ABA3
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Essential pCSC subunit for drought/high-light signal-induced ABA biosynthesis and stomatal closure; constitutively associating engineered variant enhances drought tolerance
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. oastlb mutants fail to close stomata or induce ABA reporter in response to sulfate, CLE25, or OPDA, but respond to exogenous ABA. 2. AtOAS-TL B complementation restores wild-type drought responses. 3. Engineered OAS-TL B(M167A), which forms an OAS-resistant stable pCSC, elevates OAS/cysteine, increases ABA, reduces stomatal aperture, and improves drought tolerance without growth penalty.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; complementation; transgenic overexpression of engineered alleles; in vitro pull-down CSC dissociation assay; enzyme assay; metabolite profiling; stomatal and drought assays
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control component of pCSC assembly state; not established as sole rate-limiting enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Soil drying; sulfate; CLE25; OPDA; high light stress; OAS-mediated CSC dissociation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Stable pCSC variant caused partial constitutive stomatal closure and increased ABA, but no detectable growth or seed-yield penalty under non-stressed conditions
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OAS-TL B protein-engineering, especially OAS-dissociation-resistant allele M167A, is highlighted as an easily engineered route to drought-resistant crops
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: OAS-TL B(S172N) still allows OAS-mediated CSC dissociation and does not confer drought-tolerant phenotype, whereas M167A produces a constitutively activated pCSC.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Molybdenum cofactor sulfurylation step activating AAO3
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Immediate upstream activation step for terminal ABA formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers sulfur derived solely from cysteine to the molybdenum cofactor required to activate AAO3
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required for direct formation of ABA by activating AAO3
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Necessary for OPDA/pCSC-driven ABA production and stomatal closure; abolishing ABA3 reopens stomata in constitutive pCSC lines
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aba3 mutants fail to close stomata in response to OPDA, demonstrating ABA dependence of the OPDA response. 2. Constitutive pCSC-induced stomatal closure requires ABA3, because aba3 background reopens stomata despite high OAS/cysteine. 3. ABA3 is mechanistically linked to pCSC by using cysteine as the sulfur donor for AAO3 activation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; stomatal aperture assays; ABA reporter analysis in guard cells; genetic crosses with constitutive pCSC lines; metabolite profiling
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control step linking cysteine availability to active AAO3-dependent ABA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: OPDA treatment; high light stress; constitutive pCSC activation
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: MYB60 guard cell-specific promoter (complementation line mentioned); 35S constitutive promoter in pCSC-engineering background
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Important downstream dependency to consider when engineering pCSC-based drought resilience; sufficient ABA3 activity in guard cells is required
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: MYB60::ABA3 aba3-1 restored OPDA-induced stomatal closure, supporting guard cell-autonomous ABA biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal oxidation step of ABA formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes abscisic aldehyde to ABA using sulfurylated molybdenum cofactor
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for OPDA-induced stomatal closure, placing the pCSC output upstream of terminal ABA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. aao3 mutants fail to close stomata in response to OPDA. 2. The paper positions AAO3 as the terminal ABA-forming enzyme activated by ABA3-dependent sulfurylated MoCo. 3. pCSC promotes ABA formation partly by supplying cysteine needed for the ABA3-AAO3 module.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; stomatal aperture assays; pathway logic from genetics
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant for ABA formation in this pathway
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AAO3 activity depends on sulfurylated MoCo produced via ABA3 using cysteine as sulfur donor.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Promotes pCSC assembly upon OPDA binding
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: SERAT2;1; OAS-TL B; plastid cysteine synthase complex assembly
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Cysteine biosynthesis and downstream ABA biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes assembly/activation of the pCSC rather than transcriptional regulation
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes stomatal closure and drought/high-light stress adaptation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required specifically for OPDA- and high light-induced ABA production and stomatal closure, but dispensable for sulfate- or CLE25-induced closure
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent controller that specifically couples local OPDA/high-light signals to ABA production via pCSC formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cyp20-3 mutants fail to close stomata or induce ABA reporter in response to OPDA but retain sulfate-, CLE25-, and ABA-responsiveness. 2. Genetic suppression of the constitutively closed-stomata opr3 phenotype by cyp20-3 proves endogenous OPDA requires CYP20-3 to activate the ABA pathway. 3. CYP20-3 is presented as a stroma-localized OPDA sensor that promotes pCSC assembly.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis; stomatal aperture assays; pRAB18::GFP ABA reporter; genetic interaction with opr3 mutant; high light assays
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions as cofactor/co-receptor promoting plastid cysteine synthase complex assembly
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Discussed as an OPDA-binding chloroplast sensor; prior work suggests involvement of thioredoxin-mediated SERAT2;1 remodeling during pCSC formation.
